choiceresponse.label: Which statements are best supported by the examples across vision, tabular, and text models? (Select all that apply.)

checkboxgroup.choice: Explanations describe how a model uses information, not how the real-world outcome is generated.
A visually intuitive explanation (e.g., Grad-CAM on shoreline regions) guarantees a causal relationship.
Post-hoc explanations can be misleading if interpreted as ground truth.
Models that are more complex always produce less interpretable explanations.

div.p: Across modalities, explanations reveal model reasoning, not real-world causality. In methods developed for tabular data, like SHAP, explanations reveal which features push a prediction up or down for an individual; in methods suitable for text data like LIME, they highlight the specific words that influence a classification; and in methods developed for images like Grad‑CAM, they show the image regions the model relies on for a given label. Grad-CAM and LIME often align with human intuition, but this does not imply causal correctness. Post-hoc explanations must be interpreted cautiously, especially for complex models. Complexity does not automatically imply unintelligibility—only greater reliance on approximation.
